items.question: Who relies on vector spaces in machine learning?
items.answer: Data scientists, ML engineers and researchers use vector spaces whenever they represent examples as feature vectors, embeddings as points in high-dimensional space, or activations inside neural networks.

items.question: What is a vector space?
items.answer: A vector space is a set of vectors closed under addition and scalar multiplication that satisfies the standard axioms (associativity, commutativity of addition, distributivity, existence of a zero vector and additive inverses).

items.question: When do you first encounter vector spaces in an ML pipeline?
items.answer: As soon as raw data is encoded as numeric feature vectors—for example, stacking rows of a design matrix X—because every subsequent linear operation assumes those objects live in a vector space.

items.question: Where do subspaces appear in practice?
items.answer: Subspaces show up in PCA (a low-dimensional subspace capturing variance), in the column space of a weight matrix (reachable outputs of a linear layer), and in solution sets of homogeneous linear systems.

items.question: Why is linear independence important?
items.answer: Linear independence tells you whether a set of vectors carries redundant information; a basis must be independent so every vector has unique coordinates, which is essential for dimension counting and stable representations.

items.question: How do you check whether a subset is a subspace?
items.answer: Verify three conditions: the subset contains the zero vector, is closed under vector addition, and is closed under scalar multiplication; if any condition fails, it is not a subspace.

items.question: What happens if you include a linearly dependent vector in a basis you are constructing?
items.answer: Cause: one vector in the set is a linear combination of the others. Effect: coordinates are no longer unique, dimension is over-counted, and algorithms that assume a basis (e.g. change-of-basis, some decompositions) become ill-defined or unstable.

items.question: What happens when you project high-dimensional data onto a subspace that spans too little variance?
items.answer: Cause: the chosen subspace omits directions where the data varies strongly. Effect: reconstructed points lose discriminative structure, downstream models see blurred features, and performance drops even though the math is valid.
